nativeRange.habitat: Turbid reef slopes, lagoons, and protected reef zones; tolerates lower-light, sediment-rich environments more readily than many LPS corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Benefits from supplemental target feeding 1-3 times per week with mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, or coral-specific foods. More active feeder at night; corals have been documented grabbing and consuming meaty foods surprisingly effectively. Turn off flow during feeding for best results.

avoidWith: other aggressive LPS corals placed without adequate spacing
any coral within sweeper tentacle reach (15+ cm clearance needed)
strong direct flow equipment positioned toward the coral

summary: Pectinia is a genus of LPS reef corals with dramatic ruffled, leaf-like plates in vivid colors. Intermediate care; moderate light and flow, but sweeper tentacles require generous spacing from neighbors.
careNotes:
Note: Pectinia is discussed at the genus level in the aquarium trade because the ~10 recognized species share near-identical husbandry requirements. Commonly sold trade morphs include 'Space Invader Pectinia' (P. alcicornis colorforms) and the traditional 'Spiny Cup' form. The Space Invader morph has been reported to be more aggressive with extended sweeper tentacle reach than standard morphs — allow 20+ cm clearance for those.

Taxonomy note: LiveAquaria and some older sources list Pectinia under family 'Pectiniidae', but current molecular-based taxonomy (WoRMS, Wikipedia, Merulinidae reclassification studies 2014+) places the genus in Merulinidae. Merulinidae is used here.

Lighting: Pectinia tends to thrive at moderate PAR (75–150). Unlike many LPS corals, it naturally inhabits more turbid, lower-light reef zones, so it tolerates and often prefers conditions that would be considered dim for other stony corals. New specimens should be acclimated gradually over 2–3 weeks to avoid bleaching from light shock.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is generally advised. Direct laminar flow against the fleshy tissue can tear or dislodge the coral; flow that is too low allows detritus to settle in the valleys between the ridges, leading to tissue recession. Aim for gentle oscillating movement that keeps the polyps swaying without overwhelming them.

Aggression: Sweeper tentacles commonly extend 5–10 cm at night to capture food and defend territory. Maintain at minimum 15 cm of clear space around all edges of the coral. Despite this, LiveAquaria describes the species as 'peaceful' in the context of reef-fish compatibility — the aggression is directed at neighboring corals, not tankmates.

Water chemistry: Stability is generally prioritized over absolute precision. Alkalinity swings of more than 0.5 dKH per day have been linked to tissue recession. Consistent calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), and magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm) support healthy skeletal growth.

Nutrients: Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–15 ppm, phosphate 0.03–0.10 ppm) appear to support better coloration and growth than ultra-low-nutrient systems.

Cross-source note on care level: Sources ranged from 'moderate' (AlgaeBarn) to 'difficult' (LiveAquaria, Top Shelf Aquatics), with 'intermediate-to-advanced' from Whitlyn Aquatics. The consensus center is intermediate — Pectinia is not beginner-friendly but is achievable in a stable, established reef without the extreme precision demands of SPS corals.

Brown jelly disease and tissue recession are the most commonly reported health issues; both are typically triggered by physical damage, parameter swings, or excessive flow.

Fragging is possible with a coral saw or bone cutters; cut pieces readily encrust and grow on frag plugs.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a coral bandsaw; small frags encrust on frag plugs and grow readily under the same conditions as the parent colony. Sexual reproduction on natural reefs follows the standard broadcast spawning pattern for Scleractinia, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics – Complete Pectinia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–77°F / 23.9–25°C), calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm), PAR (80–150), flow (gentle-medium), placement (middle-lower), temperament (aggressive sweeper tentacles). Agreed closely with Extreme Corals on all high-stakes parameters.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Care Requirements of Pectinia Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F, salinity SG 1.024–1.026, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 80–150. Temperature upper bound (80°F) is slightly higher than other sources; averaged to 26°C max.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – ORA Pectinia Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (32–35 ppt = SG 1.025–1.027), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (350–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm). PAR suggestion (~50) is notably lower than other sources (80–150); likely reflects this ORA aquacultured strain's lower-light adaptation. PAR range set to 75–150 per majority consensus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Pectinia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for current accepted taxonomy (family Merulinidae, order Scleractinia) and genus-level species count (10 recognized species). LiveAquaria uses the outdated 'Pectiniidae'; Merulinidae reflects current molecular-based classification.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Pectinia Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked flow (moderate), PAR (~100), feeding frequency (2–3× weekly), temperament (generally non-aggressive except Space Invader morph which is 'very aggressive'). Confirmed family Merulinidae.
